text: The sense is probably, ‘I gained nothing in return when I sent you into exile at Babylon’ (cf. 50.1 above, Ps. 43.13); ‘I did not engage the gratitude of the Chaldeans, who remain idolaters; I am free therefore, to remit your sentence of exile whenever I will’. The interpretation, ‘You were sent into exile for no fault of your own, and you shall be reprieved for no merits of your own’ is neither probable in itself nor suited to the context.

‘What needs it, then or now?’ Literally, ‘And now, what is to me here?’—though this is less accurate as a rendering of the Hebrew text. The idiomatic sense which this phrase commonly has (cf. 22.16 above, and many other passages) would be ‘And what business have I to interfere here?’ But this is evidently inappropriate, and it is best to take the words literally, as in Gen. 19.12. Cf. note on verse 3.

‘Purify’; literally ‘sprinkle’, but wherever this word occurs elsewhere, the thing, not the person, is its object (i.e. you sprinkle something on a person), and various attempts have been made to amend the Hebrew text, e.g. ‘startle’. The end of this verse, in the Hebrew text, will equally well yield the sense, ‘they shall see that of which they had no tidings, that which they had never heard shall be made known to them’. But the other sense, which is given by the Latin version, is clearly assumed by St Paul in Rom. 15.21.
